cards.text: What are the five special properties of cardiac muscle?
What is the Frank-Starling law of the heart?
cards.answer: The force of cardiac contraction is directly proportional to the initial length of the muscle fibre (i.e., greater venous return leads to greater stroke volume)

tip.tip: Think of stretching a rubber band

cards.text: What is cardiac output and its normal resting value?
cards.answer: Cardiac output = Stroke Volume x Heart Rate; normal resting value is approximately 5 L/min

cards.text: What causes the first and second heart sounds?
cards.answer: S1 is caused by closure of AV valves (mitral and tricuspid) at the start of ventricular systole; S2 is caused by closure of semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonary) at the end of ventricular systole

tip.tip: Lub = AV valves, Dub = semilunar valves

cards.text: What is the Fick principle for measuring cardiac output?
cards.answer: CO = O2 consumption / (arterial O2 content - venous O2 content)

tip.tip: Uses oxygen consumption and A-V O2 difference

cards.text: How do baroreceptors regulate blood pressure?
cards.answer: Baroreceptors in the carotid sinus (CN IX) and aortic arch (CN X) detect stretch from increased BP, sending signals to the medulla to decrease sympathetic and increase parasympathetic tone, lowering HR and BP

tip.tip: Located in carotid sinus and aortic arch

cards.text: What is the sole electrical connection between atria and ventricles?
cards.answer: The AV bundle (bundle of His) — the only conducting pathway across the fibrous AV ring; damage causes heart block
Both AV and semilunar valves are closed; ventricular pressure rises rapidly with no change in volume; ends when ventricular pressure exceeds aortic/pulmonary pressure, opening the semilunar valves

cards.text: What is the effect of positive inotropic agents on the heart?
cards.answer: They increase the force of myocardial contraction; examples include sympathetic stimulation, catecholamines (adrenaline), digoxin, and calcium

cards.text: What are Starling forces in microcirculation?
cards.answer: Four forces governing fluid exchange across capillaries: capillary hydrostatic pressure (pushes out), interstitial hydrostatic pressure (pushes in), plasma oncotic pressure (pulls in), and interstitial oncotic pressure (pulls out)
